coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of porous formamidinium lead tribromide (FAPbBr3) nanowires for heavy metal ion detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shellaiah, Muthaiah
Huang, Chen-Jia
Sun, Kien Wen
coredata.dc:description: Applying porous nanostructured materials in selective metal ion quantification has become intensely researched due to their ion-capturing efficacy. Herein, porous formamidinium lead tribromide nanowires (FAPbBr3 NWs) are synthesized via the self-template directed tactic and adopted in detecting heavy metal ions via solid-state photoluminescence. The porous FAPbBr3 NWs have a length distribution between 8 μm to 13 μm with an average diameter of approximately 520 nm. Through scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and absorption/photoluminescence spectroscopy, the surface morphology, crystallinity, structures, and optical bandgap of NWs are validated. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) studies confirm the completion of NW synthesis, functional units, and elemental compositions of NWs. The porous FAPbBr3 NWs show high solid-state PL responses to Ag+ among ten metal cations at 1 mM concentration with a linear PL intensity change between 0 to 1 mM and a detection limit (LOD) of 12.23 µM. This unique solid-state metal ion sensing technique could advance future development in NWs-based sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskites
Formamidinium lead tribromide
Self-template-directed synthesis
Porous nanowires
Doping/substitution
Metal ions detection
Solid state sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of lead precursor NWs (chemical formula: HSCH2CH(NH2)COO-Pb-OH)
Solid-state PL spectral measurement of porous FAPbBr3 NWs response to metal ions
Synthesize and mechanism of FAPbBr3 NW formation
SEM and TEM investigations of precursor NWs and porous FAPbBr3 NWs
HRTEM-based selected area electron diffraction (SAED) measurement
Absorption, PL, and bandgap of porous FAPbBr3 NWs
Solid-state PL selectivity of porous FAPbBr3 NWs towards cations

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan and SPROUT project of NYCU for financially supporting this research under the contract MOST 110-2112-M-A49-029-, MOST 111-2112-M-A49-031-, MOST 110-2811-M-A49-543, MOST 111-2811-M-A49-528, and 112W84100.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route for preparing (A) lead precursor NWs (B) porous FAPbBr3 NWs and (C) photo images of porous FAPbBr3 NWs in solution and dry powder form.

(A) SEM image of lead precursor NWs (Scale bar = 20 µm); (B) SEM image of porous FAPbBr3 NWs without dilution (Scale bar = 10 µm); (C) SEM image of single porous FAPbBr3 NW (scale bar = 5 µm) (D) HRTEM image of porous FAPbBr3 NWs (scale bar = 1 µm).
Powder XRD spectrum of (A) Pb precursor NWs and (B) porous FAPbBr3 NWs.

(A) HRTEM image of a single porous FAPbBr3 NW (scale bar = 0.5 µm) (B) interplane distance of a single porous FAPbBr3 NW (d = 0.302 nm; scale bar = 5 nm) and (C) assigned (200) diffraction plane from the determined interplane distance.
FTIR spectra of L-Cysteine, Pb precursor NWs, and porous FAPbBr3 NWs.
(A) absorption and PL spectra of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs and (B) Tauc plot fitting of the absorption spectrum to determine the optical bandgap.
PL intensity changes of porous FAPbBr3 NWs before and after dropping 1 mM of different cations.

(A)-(F) Changes in PL spectra of porous FAPbBr3 NWs before and after dropping of Ag+ between 0 to 1 mM with an increment of 0.2 mM (G) Bar graph of the PL changes of porous FAPbBr3 NWs before and after dropping diverse concentrations of Ag+ ion and (H) Linear regression curve of I/I0 as a function of Ag+ concentration (in mM) for LOD calculation.

(A) SEM image of porous FAPbBr3 NWs after dropping Ag+ ions and (B) EDX elemental analysis of porous FAPbBr3 NWs after dropping Ag+. Inset shows the detected elements and corresponding percentages in the spectrum.
Schematic of doping and substitution mechanisms of interactions between porous FAPbBr3 NWs and metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Al3+, Mn2+ and Cd2+).
Comparative study on Ag+ sensing performance of FAPbBr3 NWs.

row.entry: pyrogallol red (PGR) and 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen), in a nylon membrane

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NA = Not Available; µM = Micromole (10−6 M); µM = Millimole (10−3 M)

head.ce:title: Synthesis of porous formamidinium lead tribromide (FAPbBr3) nanowires for heavy metal ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Yang-Ming Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Yang-Ming Chiao Tung University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Yang-Ming Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Applying porous nanostructured materials in selective metal ion quantification has become intensely researched due to their ion-capturing efficacy. Herein, porous formamidinium lead tribromide nanowires (FAPbBr3 NWs) are synthesized via the self-template directed tactic and adopted in detecting heavy metal ions via solid-state photoluminescence. The porous FAPbBr3 NWs have a length distribution between 8 μm to 13 μm with an average diameter of approximately 520 nm. Through scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and absorption/photoluminescence spectroscopy, the surface morphology, crystallinity, structures, and optical bandgap of NWs are validated. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) studies confirm the completion of NW synthesis, functional units, and elemental compositions of NWs. The porous FAPbBr3 NWs show high solid-state PL responses to Ag+ among ten metal cations at 1 mM concentration with a linear PL intensity change between 0 to 1 mM and a detection limit (LOD) of 12.23 µM. This unique solid-state metal ion sensing technique could advance future development in NWs-based sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Among those hybrid nanomaterials, lead halide perovskites are unique in terms of their cost-effective production, excellent visible luminescence properties, carrier mobility, etc. [1–3]. Hybrid lead halide perovskites can be produced in zero to three-dimensional (0D – 3D) structures, which have been engaged in various optoelectronic applications, such as light emitting diode (LEDs), solar cells, photodetection, ion sensors, biomedical applications, etc. [4–9]. Compared with conventional 3D and 2D-perovskite nanostructures, 1D-perovskite nanowires (NWs) display unique optical and electronic properties, which are strongly correlated to their reduced dimensionality, chemical compositions, larger surface area-to-volume ratio, better charge confinement, and anisotropic geometry [10,11]. Moreover, 1D-NWs can exhibit excellent compositional and structural flexibility, adjustable energy gaps, strong quantum confinement effects, and superior environmental stability [12,13]. Thus, research on using 1D-hybrid perovskite NWs has attracted much attention recently.

1D-perovskite nanostructures can be synthesized by the following tactics: (1) solution phase method which can be further divided into (a) one-step synthesis and (b) two-step synthesis); (2) vapor deposition method; (3) hot-injection method; (4) solvothermal method; (5) ligand-assisted reprecipitation (LARP) tactic; and (6) template-directed synthesis [14–22]. Among these tactics, the template-directed synthesis can be achieved via the following two methods: (i) anodized aluminum template method (AAO), in which anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) templates are introduced to synthesize the perovskite nanowires [23]; and (ii) self-template-guided auxiliary method, where a precursor is used as a template for self-assembly via coordinating a specific ion [24,25]. The self-template-directed synthesis is the most effective and is the most commonly used technique for fabricating 1D-NWs for diverse applications among the aforementioned methods.

For example, Zhou and co-workers successfully synthesized the porous methylamine lead bromide (MAPbBr3) NWs for fabricating visible light photodetectors [26]. They firstly synthesized The Pb-containing precursor NWs were firstly synthesized followed by reacting with CH3NH3Br (MABr) and HBr to produce the porous MAPbBr3 NWs. The as-synthesized porous NWs displayed a photo response time of 0.12 s and a decay time of 0.086 s. The NW’s resistance was determined as 6.67 × 108 Ω under the illumination of 27.5 mW with good ohmic contact. More recently, Sun et al. demonstrated current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of a single porous MAPbBr3 NW from 300 K to 83 K using a four-point probe measurement to exclude the contact resistance. The single porous NW displayed an average conductivity of 57.80 ± 4.8 S/cm, a hole concentration and mobility of 5.98 × 1018 cm−3 and 60.33 cm2 V−1 S−1, respectively, at room temperature [27]. Moreover, electrochemical sensing devices based on a single porous NW showed a stronger response to the Fe3+ than that of other ions (Cu2+, Co2+, and Hg2+). This result suggests the feasible solid-state PL sensory towards heavy metal ions by adopting the porous perovskite NWs.

The 1D-porous NWs with high porosity and specific surface area can enhance interactions between ions, electrons, and holes [27–29]. Furthermore, these 1D--porous NWs having more active sites than those of NWs with smooth surface will increase the metal ions interactions on the NW’s surface [30]. To maintain the ecosystem, detection and quantification of hazardous metal ions becomes vital [31–33]. Thus, numerous nanomaterials have been developed and reported as metal ion sensors [34,35]. The successful demonstration of adopting the 1D porous MAPbBr3 NWs as the Fe3+ selective electrochemical sensors [27] pointed to a feasible direction for designing solid-state PL-based metal ion sensors [36–38]. Although solid-state PL-based sensory responses have been demonstrated in numerous nanomaterials [39–42], however, the employment of porous 1D-NWs in this research area may bring future advances in developing photocatalytic and optoelectronic sensors [43–45]. Moreover, the optoelectronic properties of such 1D materials can be adjusted via surface modifications. For example, surface-treated SnO2 nanorods and Ar+ beam irradiated Ni NWs show enhanced photocatalytic and conductivity properties [46,47]. The main focus of this report is (i) to develop the novel porous FAPbBr3 NWs (ii) to validate the solid-state photophysical and crystalline properties and (iii) to demonstrate its solid-state applicability toward heavy metal ion quantification.

In this report, the porous 1D-FAPbBr3 NWs were synthesized using a self-template-directed tactic to demonstrate solid-state PL responses towards heavy metal ions. The surface morphology, crystallinity, dimensions, compositions, functional units, and optical bandgap of the porous NWs were carefully examined and determined using XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS, FTIR, and absorption/PL measurements. The solid-state PL responses of the NWs in the presence of diverse cations (Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cd2+ in solution at 1 mM concentration) were studied in detail. The porous FAPbBr3 NWs showed a distinct solid-state PL selectivity to Ag+ with linear regression behavior and an excellent detection limit (LOD).

Formamidinium bromide (99 %; CH(NH2)2Br (FABr)) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Lead(II)nitrate (99 %; Pb(NO3)2), ethanolamine (98 %; HOCH2CH2NH2) and hydrobromic acid (47 – 49 %; HBr) were purchased from Alfa-Aesar. L-cysteine (98 %; C3H7NO2S), isopropanol (98 %; (CH3)2CHOH (IPA)), ethanol (95 %; C2H5OH), and acetone (99.5 %; CH3COCH3) were purchased from Acros, J.T.Baker, Honeywell and UR companies, respectively.

The porous 1D-FAPbBr3 NWs were synthesized in an EJS-1250S glove box (purchased from Yujie Company, Taiwan) maintained in an inert N2 atmosphere. To prevent decomposition of the moisture-sensitive perovskite NWs, the completed samples were all kept in the EJS-1250S glove box before use. A Bruker D8 Advance X-ray Diffractometer equipped with a Vantec-1 detector and a Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) radiation source (at Department of Applied Chemistry, National Yang-Ming Chiao Tung University (NYCU)) was used to conduct the Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) experiments with scanning 2θ angles from 10° to 60°. SEM images and EDS spectra (elements at Be4∼U92 full energy spectrum) were recorded using a Hitachi SU-8100 scanning electron microscope (located at Nano center of NYCU; probe voltage range: 0.1 KV to 30 KV; maximum resolution =10 Å; maximum magnification can reach 150,000 times.). TEM images were collected from a field emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEGTEM; JEOL JEM-2100F, located at the Nano center of NYCU). The TEM instrument has an acceleration voltage of 200 KeV, a magnification of x2000–1500000, a point imaging resolution of 0.23 nm, and a lattice imaging resolution of 0.14 nm. Perkin Elmer Instruments Spectrum One Fourier Transform Near Infrared (FT-NIR) system was used to measure the FTIR spectra. Absorbance spectra of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs were obtained from a Hitachi U-4100 UV/visible/infrared (240–2600 nm) spectrometer equipped with a homogeneous light source., photomultiplier tubes (UV/Vis) and PbS (NIR) photodetectors. The PL spectra were recorded using a combination of a 0.5 m spectrometer, a PMT photodetector, and an excitation source of a continuous wave solid-state laser operated at 355 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lead precursor NWs (chemical formula: HSCH2CH(NH2)COO-Pb-OH)
ce:section.ce:para: The Pb precursor NWs were prepared by modifying the method reported earlier [26]. As illustrated in Fig. 1(A), approximately 0.033 g of Pb(NO3)2 and 0.012 g of L-cysteine were firstly dissolved in 30 ml of DI water followed by stirring on a hot plate at 700 rpm till the solution became transparent (roughly 10 min). After adding 200 μl of ethanolamine to the solution, the color immediately changed to milky white. The solution was further stirred for 20–30 min at 700 rpm followed by gravity filtration or suction filtration to obtain the precipitate containing the lead nanowire precursor. The precipitate was rinsed several times with DI water and ethanol for 2 s. The obtained lead NW precursor was kept in a hot oven at 50 °C for overnight before use.

As shown in Fig. 1(B), the dried lead NW precursor NWs were dissolved in 5 ml of IPA solution to afford the mixed lead NW precursor solution. Subsequently, FABr/IPA solution was prepared by dissolving 0.11 g of formamidinium bromide (FABr) in 10 ml of IPA followed by adding10–20 μL of HBr (μL = microliter) and stirred till the solution became transparent and clear. The completed FABr/IPA solution was injected into a vial containing the lead NW precursor solution using a dropper. The color of solution changed instantly to orange at the moment of injection. The mixture was further stirred at 900 rpm for 60 min to ensure complete reaction. The solution was then centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 3 min followed by rinsing with IPA. This process was repeated three times to obtain the porous FAPbBr3 NWs powder. The powder was kept at 50 °C in an oven to dry. Fig. 1(C) displays the photo images of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs in IPA and in powder form.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-state PL spectral measurement of porous FAPbBr3 NWs response to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state PL-based sensing of metal ions was conducted via the following two-step processes. Step 1: Substrate pre-treatment; A 4-inch silicon (Si) wafer was diced into small square silicon substrates with dimensions of approximately 1 cm by 1 cm using a diamond cutting knife. The Si substrates were then cleaned using neutral detergent, deionized water, ethanol, acetone, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) in an ultrasonic oscillator for 5–10 min, subsequently. The Si substrates were blown dry with a nitrogen gun to remove residual organic solvent or water vapor on the surface of. Step 2: Sensory measurement; 0.02 g of the dried porous FAPbBr3 NWs powder was placed evenly on the cleaned Si substrate using a spatula. 5 μL of ten metal cation solutions, each containing different cations (Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Al3+, Mn2+, and Cd2+) with a concentration of 1 mM, were prepared by mixing 100 μL of 10 mM cation solution with 900 μL of IPA. The metal cation solutions (with 1 mM concentration) were then drop-casted individually over the Si substrate surface covered with evenly spread porous FAPbBr3 NWs. The samples were kept in a glove box for at least 10 s to ensure a complete reaction before subjecting to solid-state PL measurements. By following similar procedures, six Ag+ solutions with concentrations of 0 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.8 mM, and 1.0 mM, respectively, were prepared for conducting the solid-state fluorescence experiments (under 355 nm excitation) to record and determine the linear regression and LOD.

For conducting the single NW SEM and EDS measurements, lead precursor NWs and porous FAPbBr3 NWs were firstly dispersed in IPA and then diluted twice before drop casting on Si substrates. The samples were dried at 50 °C in a nitrogen-filled glove box before investigations. To prepare samples for TEM experiments, 5 µL of diluted FAPbBr3 NWs in the IPA sample was drop-casted on surface of Cu/C grids (Carbon Type-B, 200 mesh, Cu; TED PELLA, INC., CA, USA). The grids were dried at 50⁰ C in a hot air oven for 30 min followed by keeping in vacuum for 8 hr to remove solvent residues. All samples (cast on Cu/C grids) were kept in a vacuum to avoid moisture deposition before placing in the TEM sample holder. Finally, TEM images were recorded using a JEOL-JEM-2100F (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) transmission electron microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize and mechanism of FAPbBr3 NW formation
ce:section.ce:para: The porous FAPbBr3 NWs were successfully fabricated followed the procedures illustrated in Sections 2.3, 2.4 and Fig. 1(A)-1(C). The mechanism of the formation of porous FAPbBr3 NWs is described as follows. After adding the lead precursor NWs into the HBr and FABr solution, a thin FAPbBr3 perovskite layer is initially formed on the surface of the lead precursor NWs which prevents further interactions between the precursor NWs and excess HBr and FABr, thereby maintaining the 1D-NW shape. Subsequently, the L-cysteine in the lead precursor NWs is released into the solvent and results to the formation of pores on the NWs. The pores on the NWs allow the HBr and FABr to further penetrate and react with the Pb2+ in the NWs, thereby gradually transforming the rigid shape into a porous structure. A detailed schematic of the porous FAPbBr3 NW formation is illustrated in Figure S1 (in Supporting Information (SI)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and TEM investigations of precursor NWs and porous FAPbBr3 NWs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the morphology and dimensions of the as-synthesized NWs, SEM measurements were conducted. Fig. 2(A) shows the SEM images of the lead precursor NWs obtained by self-assembly of PbO and L-Cysteine. It can be clearly seen that the NW surface is smooth and free of attachments. The lead precursor NWs have a length and diameter distribution of approximately 8–20 µm and 400–800 nm, respectively. The SEM image of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs without dilution is shown in Fig. 2(B), which displays clustering nature of the porous NWs.

On the other hand, surface of the FAPbBr3 NWs displays a high degree of porosity as reported earlier [26,27]. The porous NWs have an average length and diameter of 12.1 µm and 520 nm, correspondingly. Fig. 2(C) shows SEM image of a single porous FAPbBr3 NW after proper dilution. Finally, the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2(D) further attests the completion of the reaction and the 1D porous nature of NWs. The compositions of NWs were examined using the EDS analysis. As shown in Figures S2(A)-S2(D) (in SI), the atomic compositions of Pb and Br are estimated as 24.88 % and 75.12 %. The result is consistent with the 1:3 ratio of Pb:Br of the FAPbBr3 NWs and confirms the complete transformation from the lead precursor NWs to the porous FAPbBr3 NWs.

Crystallinity of the lead precursor NWs and porous FAPbBr3 NWs was studied using the PXRD analysis as illustrated in the following. Fig. 3(A) shows the XRD spectrum of the lead precursor NWs with diffraction peaks at 2θ = 10.79°, 18.47°, 28.49°, 43.98°, 48.12°, and 50.83°, respectively, which correspond to the (002), (010), (014), (024), (018), and (026) lattice planes. These observed diffraction peaks agree with the earlier report [26], thereby confirming the complete formation of lead precursor NWs. The PXRD spectrum of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs shown in Fig. 3(B) displays peaks at 2θ = 14.81°, 20.99°, 29.83°, 33.43°, 36.71°, 42.64°, 45.37°, 52.97 °, 55.25°, and 57.46°, which can be correlated to (100), (110), (200), (210), (211), (220), (300), (311), (222) and (321) crystal planes, respectively, Note that the PXRD results are also in good agreement with earlier reports [48]. The above PXRD results authenticate the successful and complete transformation from the lead precursor NWs to the porous FAPbBr3 NWs.

The interplanar spacing (d-spacing) can be determined based on the PXRD results by using Bragg’s Eq. (1) [48]:
(1) 2d sin(θ) = nλ,
where n is an integer (the diffraction number or the order of X-rays), λ represents the wavelength of the incident X-ray. Table S1 (in SI) summarizes the calculated d-spacing values using an X-ray wavelength of 1.5418 Å and the diffraction angles (2θ) from the XRD data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HRTEM-based selected area electron diffraction (SAED) measurement
ce:section.ce:para: A selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of a single porous FAPbBr3 NW is recorded using a HRTEM as shown in Fig. 4(A). A lattice plane spacing (d-spacing) value of 0.302 nm is calculated as shown in Fig. 4(B), which agrees well with the d-value (0.299 nm) correlated to the (200) plane (shown in Fig. 4(C)) in Table S1.

To identify the functional units of FAPbBr3 NWs and to ensure complete reaction, FTIR measurements were conducted on the L-Cysteine, lead precursor NWs and porous FAPbBr3 NWs. These measurements are also essential for justifying the sensory mechanism which will be discussed in later sections. As shown in Fig. 5, the existence of -COO and free thiol (-SH) functional units of L-Cysteine can be identified from the corresponding peaks at 1575 cm−1 and 2550 cm−1, respectively, which is also consistent with the earlier literature [49].

For the lead precursor NWs, the FTIR peaks at 1578 cm−1 and 3097 cm−1, are assigned to the COO- and -NH stretching, correspondingly. Moreover, the absence of peaks related to the L-cysteine (in the range of 2600–3100 cm−1) suggests the complete formation of the lead precursor NWs. It also indicates that the functional units of L-cysteine involved significantly in the precursor NW formation process. For the porous FAPbBr3 NWs, the FA+ displays strong characteristic peaks at 1715 cm−1, and 1580 cm−1, which are assigned to the -CN symmetrical stretching and the N-H bending, respectively. The four peaks at 3407 cm−1, 3348 cm−1, 3265 cm–1, and 3166 cm−1 belong to the N-H symmetric stretching of the FA+ cations, which are also consistent with the earlier report [50]. Table S2 (in SI) summarizes the peak assignments from the FTIR spectra of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption, PL, and bandgap of porous FAPbBr3 NWs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optical bandgap, UV–visible absorption and PL experiments were conducted on the porous FAPbBr3 NWs deposited on an indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrate. As displayed in Figures S3(A) (in SI) and 6(A), the absorption edge of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs occurs at 536.6 nm. Therefore, a bandgap value of 2.2 eV [51] is determined for the porous FAPbBr3 NWs using the Tauc plot fitting as shown in Fig. 6(B).

PL spectra of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs were recorded with an excitation light source operated at 355 nm. As shown in Figs. S3(B) and 6(A), a sharp PL emission centered at 551 nm with a full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of approximately 29 nm is observed, which agrees well with the absorption result. To attest the solid-state utility of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs, solid-state PL response towards diverse metal ions experiments were conducted as described next.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-state PL selectivity of porous FAPbBr3 NWs towards cations
ce:section.ce:para: Structures of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs will be decomposed when dissolved in DMF or DMSO solvents. On the other hand, the NW samples are also insoluble in ethanol, acetone, or IPA. Thereby, the solid-state PL investigations of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs towards selected metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Al3+, Mn2+, and Cd2+ ions) have to be conducted in DI water with a concentration of 1 mM. To obtain accurate PL responses to each cation, two separate PL measurements, namely before and after metal ions exposure, were recorded subsequently. The integrated intensity of the two PL spectra were compared to obtain the relative changes. As displayed in Fig. 7, the solid-state PL intensity (I/I0) changes (before and after metal ions exposure) indicate a higher selectivity to Ag+ ions than that of other ions.

The individual PL intensity (I/I0) changes of Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Al3+, Mn2+, and Cd2+ are calculated as 0.843, 0.678, 0.433, 0.702, 0.649, 0.378, 0.258, 0.780, 0.757, and 0.666, respectively, as displayed in Fig. 7 and S4 -S8 (in SI). Be noted that, except the Fe3+ and Cr3+, the rest ions do not show any significant PL intensity changes. These observations suggest that diverse mechanisms are involved in the sensory studies, which will be explained later.

Since the porous FAPbBr3 NWs displayed high selectivity to Ag+, a separate titration between porous FAPbBr3 NWs and Ag+ was conducted to determine the regression and LOD. When adding 0 – 1 mM of Ag+ ions in IPA with an increment of 0.2 mM, the solid-state PL responses of porous FAPbBr3 NWs decrease linearly with increasing Ag+ concentrations. The values of the PL intensity changes are 0.838 (16.2 %), 0.698 (30.2 %), 0.569 (43.1 %), 0.429 (57.1 %), 0.381 (61.9 %), and 0.258 (74.2 %) at Ag+ concentrations of 0 mM, 0.2 mM, 0.4 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.8 mM, and 1 mM, correspondingly, as shown in Fig. 8(A) – 8(G). By plotting [Ag+] as a function of I/I0, a linear regression of Ag+ detection is observed between 0 – 1 mM (y = −57.06829x + 81.42314, R2 = 0.98153), as seen in Fig. 8(H). Based on the linear plot, a LOD value of 12.23 µM for the Ag+ is determined using the Eq. (2),
(2) LOD=3σ/slope,
where the ‘σ' and slope represent the standard deviation and the slope of the regression line, respectively. These results confirm the sensing utility of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs to Ag+.

The underlying mechanisms of Ag+ sensing by the porous FAPbBr3 NWs were investigated by conducting SEM and EDX measurements on NWs after interacting with Ag+. The SEM image shown in Fig. 9(A) shows that the crystal structure and morphology of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs still maintain intact after interacting with Ag+. However, the EDX spectrum taken from NWs (Fig. 9(B)) after interaction shows slight changes in atomic percentages of Pb and Br, from 24.88 % and 75.12 % before interaction to 26.58 %, and 70.30 % after interaction, respectively. More importantly, a small percentage of Ag with a value of 3.12 % is also detected. This suggests that the silver ions are able to migrate into the NWs, which could possibly result to changes in PL intensity.

In an early report on the transport-based electrochemical sensing of the porous MAPbBr3 NWs to various metal ions [27], it was found that those ions either were effectively doped in the NW (Cu2+ and Fe3+) or were able to substitute the Pb2+ in the wire (Co2+ and Hg2+), resulting to different I-V measurement results. Our SEM and EDX results of the NWs after interacting with Ag+ did not display any significant structural, morphology, or composition change, hence ruled out the possibility of Pb2+ of the NWs been substituted by the infiltrated Ag+. Thereby, the observed fluoresce quenching with Ag+ cannot be attributed to structural, morphological, or composition changes of NWs. Note that the changes observed in solid-state PL and SEM are due to the surface modification of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs induced by Ag+. Similar to the case of C+ and Ar+ beam-irradiated Ag NWs and Ni NWs, the distinct surface morphologies after irradiation resulted in enhanced conductivity [52,53].

On the other hand, Ag0 and Ag2+ ions can be produced via redox reactions when dissolving Ag+ in aqueous solution, IPA solution, or N2O solution [54]. The absorption spectrum of the solution containing the Ag0 and Ag2+ display two distinct absorption maximums at 360 nm and 310 nm for the Ag0 and Ag2+, respectively. Keep in mind that PL of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs after interacting with Ag+ were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm, which overlapped with the absorption maximum of Ag0, thereby resulting to fluorescence quenching of the NWs due to the competing absorption between the porous FAPbBr3 NWs and Ag0.

On the other end of the spectrum, the moderate fluorescence quenching of porous FAPbBr3 NWs in the presence of Fe3+ and Cr3+ can be attributed to the partial/moderate doping of ions according to the model proposed in Ref. [27]. Likewise, insignificant PL quenching of FAPbBr3 NWs to the other ions, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Mn2+, and Cd2+, is due to the substitution of Pb2+ in the perovskite NWs as suggested in Ref. [27]. A schematic and summary of the proposed underlying mechanisms involved in ion detection is illustrated in Fig. 10.

Finally, a sensory experiment was conducted to further attest applicability of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs to Ag+ by making stock Ag+ solutions from the tab water, lake water at NYCU, sea water, and drinking water, respectively. The above stock solutions were processed via the IPA-based dilution method described in the experimental section. The selectivity of Ag+ in diverse water environments was investigated following the aforementioned experimental procedure. The PL intensity changes (I/I0) of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs to Ag+ in solutions spiked from tab water, lake water at NYCU, sea water, and drinking water are between 0.2 to 0.4, thereby justifying the excellent selectivity of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs to Ag+.

As summarized in Table 1, the solid-state detection method demonstrated here using the porous FAPbBr3 NWs is not only unique but also displays reliable performance (with a linear solid-state PL response between 0 – 1 mM and a LOD of 12.23 µM) compared to the other tactics [55–65]. For example, the rhodamine 6 G (Rh6G)-based sensor, platinum complex, Schiff base probe 1, triazole-coupled benzimidazole (S1), nitrogen-rich carbon dots (NCDs), and pyrene-pyridine conjugate 1 for detecting Ag+ via fluorescence response showed a linear range at micromolar (µM) level with LODs of micro/sub-micromolar levels [56,57,59,61,64,65]. Similarly, the pyrogallol red (PGR) and 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen) in a nylon membrane, graphene oxide-platinum nanocomposite, and benzothiazole calix[4]arene (CU1)-based Ag+ colorimetric/optical detection displayed a linear range from micromolar to millimolar and LODs at micro/sub-micromolar levels [58,60,63]. Moreover, the As2S3 ion-selective membrane and modified hanging mercury drop electrode (HMDE) towards Ag+ quantification showed a micromolar to millimolar linear range and LODs at micro/sub-micromolar levels [55,62]. The above comparisons suggest that the current work reports the reliable linear regression and LOD of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs. Further optimization of the electrochemical device using this material to lower the linear regression and LODs down to nano/picomole will be the subjects of a future study.

Novel porous formamidinium lead bromide nanowires (FAPbBr3 NWs) with a length distribution of 8 μm-16 μm and an average diameter of 520 nm, respectively, are synthesized using a template-directed method and utilized in detecting heavy metal ions. High luminescent quenching selectivity of the porous FAPbBr3 NWs to Ag+ (I/I0 = 0.258) is demonstrated by comparing the PL intensity changes before and after adding analytes. Linear PL quenching behavior of Ag+ is observed between 0 – 1 mM with a LOD of 12.23 µM from the 3-sigma/slope fitting. Moreover, the sensing mechanisms when encountering various ions can be successfully interpreted via the doping/substituting and competing absorption models. Finally, this work also demonstrates the selective sensing property of porous FAPbBr3 NWs to Ag+ in real water samples. After further optimizing and assembling materials into functional devices, the sensory results of porous FAPbBr3 NWs can be adopted in solid-state discrimination of Ag+ via PL and electrochemical measurements with LODs down to nano/picomole (nM/pM).

Chen-Jia Huang: Investigation, Data curation. Kien Wen Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Muthaiah Shellaiah: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan and SPROUT project of NYCU for financially supporting this research under the contract MOST 110-2112-M-A49-029-, MOST 111-2112-M-A49-031-, MOST 110-2811-M-A49-543, MOST 111-2811-M-A49-528, and 112W84100.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.sna.2024.115960.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Qu, I. Yakub, R. Baini, W. Hu, L. Wang, Ag+ colorimetric sensor based on graphene oxide/nano-platinum composite, J. Anal. Sci. Technol. 14(1) (2023) 35.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triazole-coupled benzimidazole-based fluorescent sensor for silver, bromide, and chloride ions in aqueous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.G. Ko, W.S. Na, Mayank, N. Singh, D.O. Jang, Triazole-Coupled Benzimidazole-Based Fluorescent Sensor for Silver, Bromide, and Chloride Ions in Aqueous Media, J. Fluorescence 29(4) (2019) 945-952.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Protein-based electrochemical biosensor for detection of silver(I) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Krizkova, D. Huska, M. Beklova, J. Hubalek, V. Adam, L. Trnkova, R. Kizek, Protein-based electrochemical biosensor for detection of silver(I) ions, Environ. Toxicol. Chem. 29(3) (2010) 492-496.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paper-based cation-selective optode sensor containing benzothiazole calix[4]arene for dual colorimetric Ag+ and Hg2+ detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Phichi, A. Imyim, T. Tuntulani, W. Aeungmaitrepirom, Paper-based cation-selective optode sensor containing benzothiazole calix[4]arene for dual colorimetric Ag+ and Hg2+ detection, Anal. Chim. Acta 1104 (2020) 147-155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon dots from polyurethane: optimization using response surface methodology and selective detection of silver (I) ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.I.S.Dela Cruz, N. Thongsai, M.D.G. de Luna, I. In, P. Paoprasert, Preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon dots from polyurethane: Optimization using response surface methodology and selective detection of silver (I) ion, Colloids Surfaces A 568 (2019) 184-194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent chemosensor for silver ion at physiological pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, R. Nandhakumar, J.H. Moon, K.M. Kim, J.Y. Lee, J. Yoon, Ratiometric Fluorescent Chemosensor for Silver Ion at Physiological pH, Inorg. Chem. 50 (2011) 2240-2245.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Muthaiah Shellaiah was born in India, received his Ph.D. in Jan. 2013, and had a post-doctoral experience (5 months) under the supervision of Prof. Hong-Cheu Lin at the Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Chiao Tung University, Taiwan (ROC). Then between Sep 2014 to July 2023 (8 years and 11 months), he worked as a post-doctoral researcher on multiple projects in Prof. Kien Wen Sun’s laboratory at the Department of Applied Chemistry, National Yang-Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan (ROC). From Nov 2023 to June 2024, he was appointed as an Assistant Professor (Research) at the Department of Research and Analytics, SIMATS, Saveetha University, Chennai, India. He published more than 55 SCI research/review papers. He is also serving as a member of the topical advisory panel and currently acts as a co-guest editor in a special issue running in “Chemosensors” journal. He was recognized as an outstanding reviewer for “New Journal of Chemistry” in 2023. Presently, working on nanomaterials, chemosensors, supramolecular complexes, and organic/inorganic hybrid solar cells.

Chen-Jia Huang was born in Taiwan (ROC). He was a student who performed his graduate research at the Department of Applied Chemistry, National Yang-Ming Chiao Tung University, Taiwan (ROC) under the guidance of Prof. Kien Wen Sun. His main research focuses on the fabrication of nanomaterials-based devices.

Kien Wen Sun was born in Taipei, Taiwan. He holds a Ph.D. from the Department of Electrical Engineering at Princeton University in New Jersey, United States. From 1995 to 2000, he occupied the faculty of Electronic Engineering at Feng Chia University, Taiwan. From 2002 to 2005, he was a professor in the Department of Physics at National Dong Hwa University, Hualien, Taiwan. Since 2005, he has been a professor in the Department of Applied Chemistry at the National Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu, Taiwan. During his sabbatical in 2012, he was a visiting professor at the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at the University of Waterloo, Canada. Dr. Sun was appointed as the Department Chair of Applied Chemistry at NCTU from 2012 to 2014. He is also currently a Joint Appointment Professor at the Department of Electronics Engineering and the Director of the Center of Nano Science and Technology at National Chiao Tung University. He has published more than 100 SCI journal research/review papers in above research ﬁelds. He has served as a reviewer and editorial board member for numerous international journals. He has been engaged as a guest editor for many special issues in “Chemosensors” journal. His research interests include femtosecond laser and laser spectroscopy in III–V compound semiconductors, spintronics, nanoimprint, nanolithography, nanoelectronics, solar cells, biochips, chemosensors, and biosensing technology.
